hero.heading: Growth changes
hero.heading.subject: organisations.
hero.heading.accent: The structures, as much as the people.
hero.subtitle: I guide organisations eliminating complexity and administrative burden by designing deliberate structures, focused on what is essential, with decisions embedded so they enforce themselves. Independent of individuals, the technology in use, or sheer discipline.
whycurfee.heading.1: Simplicity is not a question of discipline.
whycurfee.body.1: Complexity does not accumulate because individuals lack discipline. It accumulates because organisations rely on discipline where structure is needed.
whycurfee.body.2: Deliberate structures cannot be designed without knowing what is already there. So I do not start with what you need. I start with what you have, and how things really look inside your organisation.
whycurfee.body.3: Structures usually grow organically rather than by design, because no one has the full picture. The same is true for organisational structure, software, and infrastructure alike. Only those who know what already exists can make deliberate decisions that prevail through the structure itself and not through individual discipline. And the more people, processes, and (AI-)tools that interact with one another, the more relentlessly complexity compounds.
whycurfee.body.4: That is the core of my work: surface reality and reduce it to what really counts. So that deliberate structures take shape, simplicity sustains itself, and your organisation can spend time and money on work that adds value, instead of drowning in complexity and holding the system together through discipline and the tireless efforts of individuals.
observations.heading.status_quo: Does any of this sound familiar?
observations.1.title: Discipline Dependency
observations.1.reality: Conventions and standards that hold only as long as someone enforces them.
observations.2.title: Structural Fragility
observations.2.reality: Dependencies and connections no one fully oversees anymore.
observations.3.title: Decisions Without Effect
observations.3.reality: Decisions were documented on paper or in wikis, but nowhere were they get enforced consistently.
observations.4.title: Knowledge Concentration
observations.4.reality: Knowledge that lives in individual minds and disappears with them.
observations.5.title: Workarounds as the Norm
observations.5.reality: What started as an exception is now the rule. No one remembers the original decision anymore.
observations.6.title: Onboarding Friction
observations.6.reality: New hires need weeks or months to find their way around the existing structures.
observations.7.title: AI-Assisted Development
observations.7.reality: AI-generated code reaching the system landscape faster than the structure can absorb it.
services.heading: How we work together
services.diagnosis.image.alt: A path through a network of connected nodes, tracing the route from symptom to root cause
services.diagnosis.title: Diagnosis
services.diagnosis.subtitle: Straight to the root cause
services.diagnosis.problem: The symptoms surface, but the root cause stays buried. Years of decisions have settled into a structure no one chose, and no one has the full picture of anymore. You want the right starting point before the next decision lands on top.
services.diagnosis.forwhom.1: You are dealing with a system landscape no one has the full overview of anymore.
services.diagnosis.forwhom.2: You change one thing, and three others break, because the dependencies between them are invisible.
services.diagnosis.forwhom.3: You face decisions whose consequences will compound over years, and you want a clear picture before you commit.
services.diagnosis.timeline.1.title: Starting from what exists
services.diagnosis.timeline.1.item.1: Clarify the concern and the structure around it
services.diagnosis.timeline.2.title: From symptom to root cause
services.diagnosis.timeline.2.item.1: Trace each symptom back to its root cause.
services.diagnosis.timeline.2.item.2: Surface what the structure has absorbed.
services.diagnosis.timeline.2.item.3: Stakeholder conversations for what the system does not reveal.
services.diagnosis.timeline.3.title: From root cause to direction
services.diagnosis.timeline.3.item.1: Findings report: root causes, impact, urgency
services.diagnosis.timeline.3.item.2: A clear recommendation for where to start.
services.diagnosis.timeline.3.item.3: A walkthrough of the findings together.
services.diagnosis.delivers.1: A compact findings report, max. five pages, ready to act on.
services.diagnosis.delivers.2: Evidence in the form required by the findings, regardless of what form that may take.
services.diagnosis.delivers.3: Causes prioritised by urgency and impact, each backed by concrete evidence from inside your organisation.
services.diagnosis.delivers.4: A clear recommendation for where to start.
services.diagnosis.excludes.1: A full audit or end-to-end mapping
services.guidance.image.alt: An unordered network of nodes on the left flowing into an ordered, honeycomb structure on the right
services.guidance.title: Guidance
services.guidance.subtitle: From root cause to solution
services.guidance.problem: The root cause is known, but the solution has not yet been designed and implemented. You know what needs to change, and you want to embed the solution so that it lasts.
services.guidance.forwhom.1: You have made decisions and written them down, but they only hold while someone is watching. People enforce them, because the structure was never built to.
services.guidance.forwhom.2: You have noticed the system taking on a shape no one intended. Small exceptions that stayed. Workarounds that became the norm.
services.guidance.forwhom.3: Decisions and changes that used to take a day now take a week, because every part of the system is connected to every other.
services.guidance.timeline.1.title: Cause
services.guidance.timeline.1.outcome: The root cause is clear before we start.
services.guidance.timeline.1.item.1: Find or deepen the root cause
services.guidance.timeline.1.item.2: Trace the dependencies that hold it in place
services.guidance.timeline.2.title: Design
services.guidance.timeline.2.outcome: The solution is defined before the first change is made.
services.guidance.timeline.2.item.2: Agree on how the changes will roll out
services.guidance.timeline.2.item.3: Align with your team before anything is built
services.guidance.timeline.3.title: Implementation
services.guidance.timeline.3.outcome: Your team implements, I make sure we stay on course.
services.guidance.timeline.3.item.1: Your team brings the expertise on your system landscape
services.guidance.timeline.3.item.2: One change at a time, until the root cause is resolved
services.guidance.timeline.3.item.3: Nothing moves on until the previous change is anchored
services.guidance.timeline.4.title: Closing
services.guidance.timeline.4.outcome: The root cause is resolved. Drift surfaces the moment it appears.
services.guidance.timeline.4.item.1: Closing session that confirms the root cause is resolved
services.guidance.timeline.4.item.2: The solution is in production and enforces itself
services.guidance.delivers.2: Standards and conventions the system enforces automatically
services.guidance.delivers.3: An implementation that stays coherent from start to finish
services.guidance.delivers.4: A solution that outlasts individuals, tools, and sheer discipline
services.advisory.image.alt: A continuous horizontal sightline connecting several evolving system networks, marking decision points across time
services.advisory.title: Advisory
services.advisory.subtitle: Coherence that grows with your system
services.advisory.problem: The decisions are yours. The time to dig into each one in depth is not. You want someone who thinks at the system level with you, and you want them within reach when you need them.
services.advisory.forwhom.1: Your system landscape keeps growing, and the decisions grow harder with it.
services.advisory.forwhom.2: Small decisions today add up to structural consequences tomorrow, and you make them without a second pair of eyes.
services.advisory.forwhom.3: Every structural decision lands on your desk, and you want someone to think them through with you.
services.advisory.timeline.1.title: Onboarding
services.advisory.timeline.1.outcome: By the end of the month, I know what matters.
services.advisory.timeline.1.item.1: Walk through the system landscape together
services.advisory.timeline.1.item.2: Understand the standards and goals currently in play
services.advisory.timeline.1.item.3: Establish the working rhythm we will use from then on
services.advisory.timeline.2.title: Feedback and alignment
services.advisory.timeline.2.item.1: Evaluate decisions in the full system context
services.advisory.timeline.2.item.2: Grounded feedback before a decision is made
services.advisory.timeline.2.item.3: The rhythm adapts to whatever lands on your desk
services.advisory.timeline.3.title: Deep dive
services.advisory.timeline.3.outcome: When the landscape shifts in a fundamental way.
services.advisory.timeline.3.item.1: Assess what has changed and what it means for the direction
services.advisory.delivers.2: Ongoing alignment that grows as your system evolves
services.advisory.delivers.3: A dedicated partner who knows the context that matters
services.advisory.delivers.4: Confidence on decisions that reach beyond your core expertise
service.forwhom.heading: Sound familiar?
service.process.heading: How a collaboration begins.
approach.heading: No consulting theatre.
approach.diagnosis.1.title: Root cause only, no symptoms
approach.diagnosis.1.body: I do not summarise symptoms. I trace each one to the root cause behind it. The findings report names the root cause itself, leaving the surface noise behind.
approach.diagnosis.2.title: Reality over relief
approach.diagnosis.2.body: The diagnosis names the reality, even if no one in the room wants to hear it. Recommendations are only effective when they sit on top of reality.
approach.diagnosis.3.title: Max. five pages, ready to act on
approach.diagnosis.3.body: The handover is a results report of max. five pages, covering the situation I started from, the root cause behind it, and a clear recommendation for the next steps.
approach.guidance.1.title: Anchored where the work happens
approach.guidance.1.body: Every standard we agree on lands where the structure can enforce it: a pipeline, a workflow, an approval gate, a schema, a runbook. If the system does not enforce it, it is not a standard.
approach.guidance.2.title: One change at a time, fully landed
approach.guidance.2.body: Nothing moves on until the previous change is in production and verified by the system itself. No half rolled out conventions.
approach.guidance.3.title: Your team builds, I keep the line straight
approach.guidance.3.body: The implementation stays with the people who know your landscape. I make sure the work does not drift from the design while it ships.
approach.advisory.1.title: Full system context on every call
approach.advisory.1.body: I learn your landscape in the first month: software, infrastructure, organisational structures, the lot. After that, every piece of feedback is grounded in what is actually in place, rather than in the slide you sent over.
approach.advisory.2.title: Honest, even when it slows you
approach.advisory.2.body: If a decision looks fast but compounds badly, I say so. The brief is structural integrity over years, even when speed today looks like the win.
approach.advisory.3.title: Reachable when it counts
approach.advisory.3.body: When a decision lands on your desk and you need a second pair of eyes the same day, that is what the rhythm is for.
fields.heading: Where root causes hide.
fields.diagnosis.1.title: Architecture no one chose.
fields.diagnosis.1.body: The architecture grew organically, not by design. Years of layered changes have accumulated, dependencies have multiplied, and the original intent has disappeared under each iteration. The diagnosis names what the system absorbed, so the next decision can be made on purpose.
fields.diagnosis.2.title: Infrastructure assembled in pieces.
fields.diagnosis.2.body: Cloud accounts, vendors, pipelines, and runtimes were each added when they made sense at the time. None of them were chosen as part of a whole. The diagnosis traces what the infrastructure has become through the work it carries.
fields.diagnosis.3.title: Structure paid in coordination.
fields.diagnosis.3.body: Approvals route through inboxes. Roles overlap, decisions stall. The organisation has pushed coordination onto people instead of absorbing it into the structure. The diagnosis names which administrative load is structural, and which is a workaround for a decision the structure never enforces.
fields.diagnosis.4.title: Between the disciplines.
fields.diagnosis.4.body: Not every root cause belongs to a single domain. Some sit in how decisions are made, who has the full picture, or what the structure has stopped enforcing. The diagnosis follows the root cause where it lives, even when the symptom appeared somewhere else entirely.
fields.guidance.1.title: Standards no system enforces
fields.guidance.1.subtitle: Conventions that hold for a week.
fields.guidance.1.body: Every team agreed on the convention in a meeting. Six weeks later, fourteen services or fourteen teams have fourteen variations. The standard does not need rewriting; it needs to be enforced where the structure can catch the deviation, inside pipelines, approval gates, workflows, or schemas, instead of by reviewers in PRs and stand-ups.
fields.guidance.2.title: Roles defined, decisions still flowing the old way
fields.guidance.2.subtitle: An org chart that nobody honours.
fields.guidance.2.body: The role definitions name who decides what. In practice, decisions still escalate to the person they used to escalate to two reorganisations ago. Guidance moves the decision rights out of the document and into the workflows, calendars, and approval surfaces where they actually take effect.
fields.guidance.3.title: Workarounds that became conventions
fields.guidance.3.subtitle: Exceptions nobody questioned.
fields.guidance.3.body: What started as one exception now shapes how every new project, ticket, or hire gets handled. Nobody remembers the original rule. Guidance traces the path back, decides which workaround stays and which one goes, and anchors the result where it cannot drift.
fields.guidance.4.title: Senior people as the enforcement layer
fields.guidance.4.subtitle: Mondays spent in review instead of design.
fields.guidance.4.body: Senior engineers, leads, or managers spend a third of every week catching the same five mistakes. Their judgement is the de facto standard, but the system does not encode it. Guidance moves that judgement out of their heads and into the structure, so it runs without them in the loop.
fields.advisory.1.title: Decision bottleneck on one desk
fields.advisory.1.subtitle: Eighty hours of decisions, forty hours to make them.
fields.advisory.1.body: Every structural decision (software, infrastructure, organisational) lands on one or two desks. Those desks have neither time to dig into each one nor anyone to delegate to. Advisory is the second pair of eyes that knows the context and shortens the path from question to grounded answer.
fields.advisory.2.title: High-stakes commitments without a sounding board
fields.advisory.2.subtitle: Nine month migration, no second opinion.
fields.advisory.2.body: You are about to greenlight a multi quarter direction: a reorg, a platform migration, a vendor lock-in, a new operating model. The last comparable commitment took twice the planned duration. Advisory pressure tests the assumption before the commit, in the full system context.
fields.advisory.3.title: Compounding small decisions
fields.advisory.3.subtitle: Today's choice, tomorrow's structure.
fields.advisory.3.body: A platform decision in one team quietly forces thirty downstream services to rewrite. A new policy in one department quietly reshapes how every hiring loop is run. The cost surfaces six months later. Advisory surfaces it before.
fields.advisory.4.title: Direction recalibration
fields.advisory.4.subtitle: When the landscape shifts.
fields.advisory.4.body: Sometimes the landscape shifts in a fundamental way, through an acquisition, a key hire, a new product line, or a regulatory change. The landscape moves faster than the architecture, the org chart, or the playbook. Advisory keeps the direction calibrated to what is actually in place and how the organisation actually runs today, rather than to last year's strategy doc.
about.heading: Philipp Höllinger
about.background.heading: What shaped my approach
about.background.lead: Context for the work I do, rather than a list of titles.
about.principles.heading: How I work
about.principles.1.text: Symptoms point the way. Causes need to be resolved. The work begins where the chain ends.
about.principles.2.text: A standard that depends on individual vigilance is a standard that drifts in the end. The system has to enforce it.
about.principles.3.text: Workarounds add up to a structure no one chose. Lay the foundation, and everything above it stays straight.
about.credentials.1.title: Roots in logistics
about.credentials.1.body: My career started in logistics, coordinating workflows between goods, people, and processes. That is where I learned that reliable structures are the difference between a process that runs and one that breaks.
about.credentials.2.title: From computer science to architecture
about.credentials.2.body: After my studies, I moved into software development, then into architecture. Across automotive, the public sector, and software consulting, I kept the big picture and the small details in view at the same time.
about.credentials.3.title: Systems in the age of AI
about.credentials.3.body: AI sees only what fits inside its context window, and brings no structure of its own. Boundaries have to be enforced by the system itself, and that only works if the structure behind it is reliable.
about.credentials.4.title: Independent practice
about.credentials.4.body: Through Curfee e.U., I work directly with founders, CTOs, and engineering managers across the DACH region. No layers in between, no account managers, only the work.
about.credentials.5.title: Decision Enforcement Principle
about.credentials.5.body: My core principle: decisions should be enforced by the system itself, through pipelines, automated checks, and tool configuration. Not through individual discipline, memory, or vigilance.
about.credentials.6.title: Based in Salzburg, Austria
about.credentials.6.body: Active across the DACH region. Remote engagements are the default; on-site visits when the work calls for it.
conway.quote: Organizations which design systems are constrained to produce designs which are copies of the communication structures of these organizations.
calltoaction.heading: Questions before you reach out?
calltoaction.subtitle: Send me a quick note about your situation, and I'll reply within three working days.
differentiator.1.title: Think about AI as a whole.
differentiator.1.body: Technology is not neutral. It changes how teams work together, how leadership operates, how decisions get made.
differentiator.2.title: No hype. No empty buzzwords.
differentiator.2.body: I will not tell you AI will change everything. I will tell you what AI can deliver in your specific situation, and where it cannot.
differentiator.3.title: People first.
differentiator.3.body: Every measure is built so that the people meant to work with AI actually want to.
about.who.heading: Founder, Curfee e.U. · Austria
about.who.1: I see patterns before they become obvious. When I look at a system, within minutes I can point out the areas that will become critical later, often before I can put the thought into words. I am only at ease when decisions no longer depend on someone enforcing them. That is what drives me.
about.who.2: At its core, it is a passion for what stays unnoticed: the small details that shape the future of a system.
about.who.3: My career started in logistics, coordinating the flow between goods, people, and processes. What stayed with me from there: when people with different ways of working have to come together, clear directions and reliable structures are essential, so that processes run smoothly and everyone can rely on them.
about.who.3b: In the age of AI, the need for structure becomes even more pressing. AI sees only what fits into its context window, and brings no structure of its own. It has no frame to operate in, follows no guidelines, and certainly respects no boundaries. The system itself has to enforce those boundaries, and that only works when the structure behind it is reliable.
about.who.4: After my degree in computer science, I moved into software development and then into architecture, with stops in automotive, the public sector, and software consulting. Architecture was the logical next step. I always wanted to keep the big picture and the small details in view at the same time, because one is not complete without the other. At some point, something became clear that has stayed with me ever since: the reliability that was a given in logistics remains the exception in software systems and the organisations that build them.
about.who.5: The more tools, processes, and people interact with one another, the more important it is for structures to enforce decisions automatically. The same is true for software, infrastructure, and organisational structures. Yet it rarely happens. Structure tends to grow organically rather than by design, because directions that enforce themselves inside the system are rarely planned from the start.
about.who.5b: Dedicated roles that carry this responsibility are rare. Instead, committed individuals step in and take it on alongside their regular duties, with considerable effort, but without the support or time to anchor their work sustainably in the system.
about.who.6: So that organisations and their engineers can spend time and money on work that matters, instead of holding the system together by hand. Structural decisions belong anchored in the system itself, enforcing themselves from there, independent of individuals, the technology in use, and sheer discipline.
faq.heading: Frequently asked questions
faq.subtitle: Your question is not here? Bring it to the first conversation.
faq.patterns.structural.question: What patterns do you see in system landscapes?
faq.patterns.structural.answer:
Patterns about the system itself, with the potential inside each:

  • Discipline Dependency to Decision Enforcement: conventions and standards that survive only as long as someone is watching become conventions and standards that enforce themselves.
  • Structural Fragility to Structural Integrity: a system landscape where complexity has made every change unpredictable becomes one where every change stays predictable as it grows.
  • Decision Drift to Embedded Decisions: decisions that exist on paper but nowhere else become decisions embedded in the system itself.
  • AI-Assisted Development to AI by Design: AI-generated code arriving faster than structure can keep up with becomes a system landscape that absorbs AI-generated code without losing its shape.
faq.patterns.operational.question: What patterns do you see in teams and organisations?
faq.patterns.operational.answer:
Patterns about how the people working inside the system experience it, with the potential inside each:

  • Knowledge Concentration to Shared Understanding: knowledge that lives in individual minds and disappears with them becomes context that stays in the organisation when people move on.
  • Coordination Overhead to Shared Foundation: individuals and teams each pulling in their own direction become individuals and teams whose work compounds rather than collides.
  • Onboarding Friction to Self-Guided Onboarding: new hires who need weeks or months to grasp the system landscape become new hires who spend their first weeks contributing rather than deciphering.
faq.decisions.question: What kinds of decisions are you talking about?
faq.decisions.answer: The Decision Enforcement Principle applies to anything that shapes how a system landscape evolves: conventions (naming, structure, layering), standards (what must hold for every service, module, or pipeline), guidelines (how the team applies those standards in edge cases), and guardrails (the automated rules that catch inconsistencies before they reach production). The same goes for organisational systems: role definitions, escalation paths, decision rights, and process boundaries are also structural decisions that need to be anchored where they actually take effect, beyond policy documents. I treat all of them as decisions that have to be anchored at a concrete point in the system, beyond documentation.
faq.enforcement.question: How do standards actually enforce themselves?
faq.enforcement.answer: Standards enforce themselves through any mechanism in the tooling landscape that runs without human intervention: CI/CD pipelines, linters, pre-commit hooks, automated tests, infrastructure as code, policy as code, schema validation, and tool configuration. In organisational systems, the same principle applies through other mechanisms: workflow automation, role definitions, escalation paths, decision-rights matrices, and onboarding pipelines. The specific mechanisms vary by system landscape; the principle stays the same. Inconsistencies surface automatically, the moment they happen, before they reach production. This is what I call the Decision Enforcement Principle.
faq.ai.question: How do you think about AI tools in this work?
faq.ai.answer: AI coding tools accelerate the very challenge I work on. An AI model only sees what fits inside its context window and brings no structure of its own, no frame, no guardrails, no boundaries. The system landscape has to enforce them. Without clear structure, AI-generated code lands in a codebase faster than anyone can take it in.
faq.cultural.question: What if the issue is cultural rather than structural?
faq.cultural.answer: Culture does not fix structure. Most of what looks cultural is individuals compensating for gaps the system leaves open. Once the structure enforces decisions, people stop carrying that weight, and what looked cultural often shifts on its own. If the root cause really is something else, the diagnosis surfaces it during the first engagement.
faq.1.question: Who do you typically work with?
faq.1.answer: I work with decision-makers responsible for a system landscape, whether software, infrastructure, or the organisation itself, where complexity has overtaken clarity: CEOs, CTOs, COOs, founders, and team leads building companies in which decisions need to outlast leadership changes, tool migrations, and team growth. The common thread is an environment that has grown organically over years, where dependencies are hard to trace and the root causes of recurring problems are not yet clear.
faq.2.question: What happens in the first conversation?
faq.2.answer: The first conversation is informal. You tell me what is on your mind, I listen. By the end of it, both of us know what the next step is.
faq.4.question: Do you build the solution yourself?
faq.4.answer: No. I design the solution and guide your team through the implementation. The actual build stays with the people who know the system landscape best. This split keeps expertise inside your company and makes the results stick once the project is finished.
faq.keep.question: What do I keep after the engagement?
faq.keep.answer:
Everything. The findings, the design, the standards, the implementation, all of it is yours. Nothing I have designed depends on me staying around afterwards. The goal is a self-sustaining system, so that you do not need further support.

  • Discipline Dependency to Decision Enforcement: conventions and standards that hold only as long as someone enforces them become conventions and standards that enforce themselves.
  • Structural Fragility to Structural Integrity: a system landscape where complexity has made every change unpredictable becomes one where every change stays predictable, even as the complexity grows.
  • Decision Drift to Embedded Decisions: decisions that exist only on paper become decisions embedded in the system itself.
  • AI-Assisted Development to AI by Design: AI-generated code that piles up faster than the structure can keep pace with becomes a system landscape that absorbs AI-generated code without losing its shape.
faq.5.question: Do you work remotely or on-site?
faq.5.answer: Both. Most of my work happens remotely, with on-site visits when the situation calls for it. The service model is remote-first, and the analysis itself does not require a constant physical presence.
faq.international.question: Do you only work in the DACH region?
faq.international.answer: I am based in Salzburg, Austria, and work primarily across the DACH region (Germany, Austria, Switzerland). Most engagements are remote, so geography is less of a constraint than it once was. I work in German and English. Teams outside DACH that share one of those languages and a workable timezone fit just as well.
faq.6.question: Do you work inside sprints or agile frameworks?
faq.6.answer: No. Structural change does not fit inside a sprint. I work alongside the team's rhythm, but the analysis and design of structural decisions need more depth and undivided focus than a single iteration allows.
blog.heading: Blog
blog.subtitle: On systems, structure, and the decisions that shape them.
blog.indexSubtitle: On systems, structure, and the decisions that shape them.
meta.blog.title: Blog | Curfee
meta.blog.description: Essays on structural architecture across software, infrastructure, and organisations, and on decision enforcement, by Philipp Höllinger, based in Salzburg, Austria.
blog.relatedSubtitle: More along the same line of thought.
blog.ctaText: If these patterns sound familiar, a conversation is often the faster way forward.
privacy.heading: Privacy Policy
privacy.phone.label: Phone
privacy.scope: This privacy policy informs users of this website, in accordance with Art. 13 of the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), about the nature, scope, and purpose of the processing of personal data by the data controller.
privacy.controller.heading: Data controller
privacy.personal.heading: Processing of personal data
privacy.personal: Personal data is collected on this website only to the extent that is technically necessary. Processing is subject to purpose limitation and data minimisation. Collected data is under no circumstances disclosed to third parties without a legal basis. The processing of personal data is based on the following legal grounds: Consent pursuant to Art. 6(1)(a) GDPR, the data subject has given consent to the processing of their personal data for one or more specific purposes. Performance of a contract pursuant to Art. 6(1)(b) GDPR, processing is necessary for the performance of a contract to which the data subject is party, or in order to take steps at the request of the data subject prior to entering into a contract. Legitimate interests pursuant to Art. 6(1)(f) GDPR, processing is necessary for the purposes of the legitimate interests pursued by the controller or by a third party, except where such interests are overridden by the interests or fundamental rights and freedoms of the data subject.
privacy.rights.heading: Rights of data subjects
privacy.rights: As a data subject, you have the following rights. Right of access (Art. 15 GDPR): you have the right to obtain confirmation as to whether personal data is being processed, as well as information about such data and its processing. Right to rectification (Art. 16 GDPR): you have the right to request the rectification of inaccurate or incomplete personal data. Right to erasure (Art. 17 GDPR): you have the right to request the erasure of your personal data, subject to the conditions laid down in Art. 17 GDPR. Right to restriction of processing (Art. 18 GDPR): you have the right to request the restriction of processing of your personal data. Right to data portability (Art. 20 GDPR): you have the right to receive your personal data in a structured, commonly used, and machine-readable format. Right to object (Art. 21 GDPR): you have the right to object at any time, on grounds relating to your particular situation, to the processing of personal data concerning you.
privacy.withdrawal.heading: Right to withdraw consent
privacy.withdrawal: Pursuant to Art. 7(3) GDPR, you have the right to withdraw consent at any time. The lawfulness of processing carried out on the basis of consent before its withdrawal is not affected. Withdrawal can be declared by email.
privacy.complaint.heading: Right to lodge a complaint
privacy.complaint: If you believe that the processing of your personal data violates the GDPR, you have the right, pursuant to Art. 77 GDPR, to lodge a complaint with the competent supervisory authority.
privacy.ssl.heading: SSL/TLS encryption
privacy.ssl: For security reasons and to protect the transmission of personal data, this website uses SSL or TLS encryption. You can identify an encrypted connection by the fact that the browser's address bar starts with 'https://'.
privacy.cookies.heading: Cookies and stored data
privacy.cookies: This website does not set any cookies and does not store any data about its visitors. There is no tracking, no profiling, and no third-party scripts that would set cookies or persist information on your device. The only data processed is what is technically required to deliver the page (see the Hosting section below).
privacy.links.heading: Links to third-party websites
privacy.links: This website contains links to third-party websites. When you follow such external links, data may be transmitted to the respective operator. The controller accepts no liability for the content and data processing by third parties. Users are encouraged to review the privacy policies of linked websites.
privacy.hosting.heading: Hosting
privacy.hosting: This website is hosted on GitHub Pages, a service provided by GitHub, Inc., 88 Colin P Kelly Jr St, San Francisco, CA 94107, USA. When this website is accessed, information is automatically recorded in so-called server log files. This includes: IP address, date and time of access, requested resources, browser and operating system used. This data is stored temporarily for security reasons and deleted after a short period. Processing is based on the legitimate interest pursuant to Art. 6(1)(f) GDPR to ensure the uninterrupted operation of the website. For the transfer of personal data to the USA (third country), GitHub uses standard contractual clauses pursuant to Art. 46(2)(c) GDPR. GitHub is also certified under the EU-US Data Privacy Framework.

imprint.liability.content.heading: Liability for the content of this website
imprint.liability.content: The media owner takes care to provide correct and current information, but accepts no liability for its accuracy, completeness, or timeliness. Should any legal infringement become known, such content will be removed without delay.
imprint.liability.links.heading: Liability for outgoing links
imprint.liability.links: This website contains links to external third-party websites whose content the media owner has no influence over. The media owner therefore accepts no liability for this external content. The respective provider or operator of the linked pages is always responsible for their content. The linked pages were checked for possible legal violations at the time of linking; no unlawful content was identifiable at that time. Permanent monitoring of the linked content is not reasonable without concrete indications of a legal violation. Should any legal infringement become known, such links will be removed without delay.
imprint.copyright.heading: Copyright
imprint.copyright: The use of texts and images from this website without explicit consent is not permitted. The reproduction, editing, distribution, and any kind of use beyond the limits of Austrian copyright law require the written consent of Curfee e.U. Where the content on this site was not created by the media owner, the copyrights of third parties are observed. In particular, third-party content is identified as such. If you become aware of a copyright infringement, please notify the media owner. Should any legal infringement become known, such content will be removed without delay.

rss.title: Curfee Blog
rss.description: Essays on structural architecture across software, infrastructure, and organisations, by Philipp Höllinger.
schema.audienceName: CTOs, engineering managers, team leads, founders
schema.audienceType: Decision-makers in companies with system landscapes that have grown over time
schema.serviceOutput: Root cause analysis, architecture standards enforced by the system through pipelines and tooling, governance frameworks, hands-on guidance through implementation
schema.decisionEnforcement.name: Decision Enforcement Principle
schema.decisionEnforcement.description: Architecture decisions are enforced by the system itself, through pipelines, automated checks, and tool configuration, instead of through individual discipline, memory, or vigilance. The core principle of Curfee.
schema.person.description: Philipp Höllinger is a structural architect based in Salzburg, Austria, and the sole founder of Curfee e.U. (founded in 2026). He advises on structural architecture across software, infrastructure, and organisational structures, on root cause analysis, and applies the Decision Enforcement Principle: architecture decisions are enforced by the system itself, through pipelines, automated checks, and tool configuration, instead of through individual discipline, memory, or vigilance.
schema.person.image.caption: Portrait of Philipp Höllinger, founder of Curfee
schema.person.skills: Structural architecture across software, infrastructure, and organisational structures, root cause analysis, dependency analysis, decision enforcement, design of standards and conventions, evaluation of tooling landscapes
schema.place.name: Salzburg, Austria
